onboarding.question:
1. Define the state transition when a logged-out user's session expires while the non-dismissible warning banner is visible — does the banner disappear and transition to the session-expired inline error, or do both states coexist?
2. Clarify whether "retry automatically" on AI unavailability (AC#16) means the user resends the exact last message or sends a new message, and whether the system automatically retries or requires explicit user action to trigger retry.
3. Define the user-facing behavior when logged-out conversation carry-over fails after successful sign-up — should the user see an explanation inline on the home screen, and can they attempt recovery or is the conversation permanently lost from their perspective?
4. Resolve the contradiction in AC#2 and AC#25 — the logged-out indicator is defined as static, non-interactive text "Not signed in," but the flows require users to tap it to open the Auth Sheet; clarify whether the indicator itself is tappable, or if sign-in CTAs appear elsewhere in the UI.
onboarding.recommendations:
[1]. My recommendation: When a logged-out user's session expires while the non-dismissible warning banner is visible, the banner is immediately replaced by the session-expired inline error message at the top of the chat ("Your session has expired. Sign in to continue") — the two states do not coexist; only one message appears at the top at any given time.

→ Rationale: A single error message at the top is clearer than overlapping states; replacing the expiry warning with the actual expiry error gives the user the actionable next step without ambiguity.

→ Note: Pending your approval — say yes to apply to the product spec

---

[2]. My recommendation: "Retry automatically" means the user sends a new message (not a resend of the last message); the system does not automatically retry on its own — the user's action of sending the next message triggers the AI to attempt a response again; if the AI remains unavailable, the same inline error reappears.

→ Rationale: Requiring explicit user action (sending a new message) prevents infinite retry loops and respects the principle that the user remains in control; sending a new message rather than resending the old one allows the user to adapt their query if needed.

→ Note: Pending your approval — say yes to apply to the product spec

---

[3]. My recommendation: When logged-out conversation carry-over fails after successful sign-up, the user lands on the authenticated home screen with an inline message at the top confirming successful sign-in and explaining that the previous conversation could not be recovered — the conversation is permanently lost from the user's perspective (no recovery mechanism is offered); the user can start a new conversation immediately.

→ Rationale: The user has already successfully signed up, so blocking them on an unrecoverable conversation would create frustration; a clear explanation maintains trust; no recovery mechanism is offered because the conversation is unrecoverable at the product level.

→ Note: Pending your approval — say yes to apply to the product spec

---

[4]. My recommendation: The "Not signed in" indicator itself is non-interactive and not tappable — it is static text only; sign-in CTAs appear elsewhere in the UI: in the session expiry warning banner ("Sign in now" button), in the in-conversation nudge (sign-up/sign-in call-to-action), in the navigation-away modal ("Sign in" button), and in the chat interface as a general affordance or discoverable via tapping the prompt bar or another UI element that opens the Auth Sheet.

→ Rationale: Keeping the indicator non-interactive reinforces its role as a passive status badge; CTAs in context-specific warnings and nudges guide the user to sign in at moments when it matters; this separation of concerns maintains the indicator's subtlety while ensuring sign-in paths are discoverable.

→ Note: Pending your approval — say yes to apply to the product spec

---

Say *yes* to apply these to the product spec and continue design, or reply to adjust any recommendation.
